environmental_storytelling.example: Stage sequence (Theater 1-3 → Junk Yard 1-3 → Toy Store 1-3 → House 1-2 + Boss) establishes narrative setting without dialogue; implies chase across distinct locations
Each zone (Theater, Junk Yard, Toy Store, House) presents contextually appropriate hazards and platform layouts that reflect real-world properties of those spaces
House stage placement at end with dedicated Boss 4 encounter signals climactic confrontation; setting matches cartoon series indoor chase tropes
Japan version Game Over screen with continue option vs. USA/EU hard reset communicates different player retention strategies tied to regional market expectations

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Movement through stage environments; collision-based interaction with world
Evasion platforming; avoidance of cat sprite contact
Cheese bit accumulation signals progression toward stage completion and warp eligibility
Patrol or pursuit initiation when player proximity detected
Active chase behavior; collision causes player damage or failure
Pattern-based attack behavior; escalated threat level signaling final confrontation

notes: Tom & Jerry (SNES) uses standard licensed platformer visual communication. World state manifests primarily through location-based settings with Tom as the consistent visual antagonist across all environments. Danger signals are minimalist (cat sprite = threat). Safety signals rely on collectibles and lives system typical of early 90s platformers. Faction distinction is simple binary (Jerry vs. Tom). Regional differences in Game Over screen represent mechanical communication variance. Source material (TCRF) focuses on development/prototype data rather than in-depth gameplay analysis, limiting detailed manifestation layer documentation. Game mechanics suggest implicit chase narrative through environmental progression without explicit HUD storytelling.
sources_used: tcrf - Tom & Jerry (SNES) - The Cutting Room Floor
